refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not a standalone FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can capture energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved) over a defined evaluation period, compute energy_per_ton, and present this metric to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance) via a reporting interface. Verify accuracy of measurement against calibrated instrumentation.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (mass moved) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and store energy efficiency metrics (energy per unit output) at defined intervals.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide authorized stakeholders access to historical and real-time energy efficiency data.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input pathway with continuous measurement device; instrument work output pathway with continuous measurement device; compute and log energy_per_ton ratio at configurable intervals; expose ratio via standardized data interface for external reporting systems.

DP2: Deploy energy metering at system boundary (total input); deploy work quantification via independent sensor on load/output; synchronize timestamps; calculate ratio post-hoc via data aggregation layer; report via API.

DP3: Embed energy accumulator in power distribution logic; embed work counter in motion control logic; periodically synchronize accumulators via message bus; compute ratio in centralized analytics module; publish to reporting interface.

DP4: Measure energy consumption via inline instrumentation on primary power feed; measure work output via independent load cell or displacement sensor; stream both signals to real-time computation engine; maintain rolling window of energy_per_ton; expose current and historical metrics via query interface.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Deploy energy metering at system boundary (total input); deploy work quantification via independent sensor on load/output; synchronize timestamps; calculate ratio post-hoc via data aggregation layer; report via API.

potential_sources: Energy and work measurement devices must share synchronized time reference (mitigated by explicit timestamp synchronization requirement).
Data aggregation layer depends on both measurement streams being available and valid (mitigated by independent sensor selection and fault detection in aggregation logic).
Reporting interface must handle missing or delayed data from either measurement pathway (mitigated by explicit data quality checks before ratio computation).
coupling_check.notes: DP2 achieves loose coupling by: (1) separating energy measurement from work measurement into independent physical pathways, (2) deferring ratio computation to a dedicated aggregation layer rather than embedding it in either measurement device, (3) exposing results via standardized API rather than proprietary protocol. Synchronization dependency is explicit and manageable. DP1 couples measurement and computation too tightly. DP3 introduces message bus coupling and distributed state. DP4 adds real-time computation complexity without clear benefit over post-hoc analysis for acceptance criteria.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy input is measurable at a single system boundary (e.g., main power feed or fuel supply line). RISK: If energy is supplied via multiple independent sources, boundary definition must be clarified or DP revised to multi-point metering.

ASSUMPTION: Work output is quantifiable via a single independent sensor (e.g., load cell, displacement, or ton-moved counter). RISK: If work definition is ambiguous or multi-modal, sensor selection and calibration will be contentious; recommend explicit work definition in FR refinement.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization between energy and work measurement devices can be achieved to within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±1 second for hourly reporting). RISK: If devices use independent clocks, drift over evaluation period may exceed tolerance; recommend GPS or NTP sync or explicit clock correction in aggregation logic.

ASSUMPTION: Post-hoc ratio computation (rather than real-time) is acceptable for acceptance criteria evaluation. RISK: If operational feedback or adaptive control requires real-time energy_per_ton, DP2 may require augmentation with streaming computation.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not mandate real-time energy monitoring for worker protection. RISK: If safety-critical decisions depend on energy_per_ton, DP2's post-hoc approach may be insufficient; escalate to safety review.

ASSUMPTION: Data aggregation layer has access to both measurement streams with sufficient frequency and reliability. RISK: Network or sensor failures could result in missing data points; recommend explicit data quality and gap-handling strategy in IM design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'work output' (e.g., 'ton of material moved horizontally 100 m', 'ton lifted vertically 10 m', or 'ton-meter of work'); (2) Evaluation period granularity (e.g., per shift, per day, per week) and acceptable reporting latency; (3) Tolerance for timestamp synchronization error; (4) Handling of partial or missing data (e.g., minimum data completeness threshold for acceptance). DP2 is robust to these refinements. Additionally, confirm with safety stakeholders whether real-time energy_per_ton feedback is required for operational decisions; if yes, DP2 should be augmented with a streaming computation path in parallel to post-hoc analysis.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, work quantification, timestamp synchronization, ratio calculation, and efficiency reporting infrastructure that spans vehicle operation and teleoperation contexts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw energy input measurements (system boundary metering) and work output sensor data (load/output quantification) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Publishes synchronized, aggregated efficiency metrics (energy ratio, work-per-joule) and diagnostic data.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits efficiency reports and API responses to external consumers (monitoring dashboards, control systems, analytics platforms).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Optional: flags anomalous efficiency ratios that may indicate mechanical failure or sensor malfunction.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for cross-system energy efficiency analysis. Metering aggregation, timestamp synchronization, and ratio calculation are core NGHE responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and API egress cross boundaries; sensor hardware ownership remains with Vehicle/Teleoperation.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns the load/output sensor and input metering hardware, but does not own teleoperation context or cross-system aggregation logic. Timestamp synchronization and ratio calculation would diffuse into Vehicle, violating separation of concerns. Vehicle should not be responsible for efficiency reporting across heterogeneous operational modes.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/command subsystem, not a data aggregation layer. Energy metering and efficiency calculation are orthogonal to teleoperation logic. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to ingest Vehicle sensor data, synchronize timestamps, and compute ratios—mixing concerns.

Poor fit. Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and alert generation, not energy efficiency analysis. While anomalous efficiency could trigger a safety alert, the core metering, aggregation, and ratio calculation do not belong in Safety Services. High diffusion and low cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (energy metering at system boundary, work quantification, timestamp synchronization, post-hoc ratio calculation, and API reporting) is fundamentally a data aggregation and analytics function that operates across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. NGHE Solution is the designated cross-system efficiency analysis layer and is the only module with sufficient scope and responsibility to contain this DP without scattering logic across multiple subsystems. Vehicle and Teleoperation own the sensors and hardware; NGHE Solution owns the aggregation, synchronization, and reporting logic.
containment_risks: Sensor ownership ambiguity: If Vehicle or Teleoperation do not reliably expose raw metering and work data via Telemetry interface, NGHE Solution will be forced to reach into subsystem internals, increasing coupling.

Timestamp synchronization complexity: If Vehicle and Teleoperation clocks are not synchronized or if Telemetry does not carry precise timestamps, NGHE Solution's post-hoc aggregation will be unreliable. Requires explicit clock synchronization protocol.

API design coupling: If external consumers expect efficiency reports in heterogeneous formats, NGHE Solution may need to maintain multiple API variants, increasing complexity. Recommend standardizing on a single canonical efficiency report schema.

Latency and buffering: Post-hoc aggregation introduces latency. If real-time efficiency feedback is required for control loops, this DP may be insufficient; consider hybrid approach with in-situ metering for fast feedback and NGHE aggregation for reporting.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP is well-formed and appropriately scoped. No revision needed. Ensure that: (1) Vehicle and Teleoperation expose raw metering and work data via Telemetry interface with precise timestamps; (2) NGHE Solution implements a robust timestamp synchronization protocol (e.g., NTP or GPS-based); (3) API schema is standardized and versioned to avoid diffusion of report formats across consumers. If real-time efficiency feedback is required for active control, consider a secondary DP that deploys lightweight in-situ efficiency estimation in Vehicle/Teleoperation, with NGHE Solution providing authoritative post-hoc validation and reporting.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly and completely addresses the FR. The DP specifies a coherent solution: (1) energy metering at system boundary captures total input; (2) independent work sensor quantifies output; (3) timestamp synchronization enables reliable pairing; (4) post-hoc ratio calculation in data aggregation layer computes energy_per_ton; (5) API reporting delivers the metric. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific sensor types or communication protocols) and is verifiable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Sensor ownership ambiguity: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems must reliably expose raw metering and work data via Telemetry interface with precise timestamps. If either subsystem withholds or delays data, NGHE Solution will be forced to reach into subsystem internals, increasing coupling and violating module boundaries.

Timestamp synchronization protocol not yet specified: Post-hoc aggregation depends on synchronized clocks across Vehicle and Teleoperation. If clock drift or jitter is not controlled (e.g., via NTP, GPS, or explicit handshake), ratio calculation will be unreliable. This is a critical DP<->IM bridge risk.

API schema standardization: If external consumers (monitoring dashboards, control systems, analytics platforms) demand heterogeneous report formats, NGHE Solution may need to maintain multiple API variants. This would diffuse the reporting logic across multiple code paths. Recommend enforcing a single canonical efficiency report schema.

Latency and real-time feedback gap: DP specifies post-hoc aggregation, which introduces latency. If active control loops require real-time efficiency feedback (e.g., for load balancing or power management), this DP alone is insufficient. A secondary in-situ metering DP may be needed in Vehicle/Teleoperation, creating a secondary IM boundary and potential logic duplication.

SafetyAlert interface coupling: Optional anomalous efficiency detection may require Safety Services to consume NGHE efficiency metrics. If Safety Services does not have a clean interface to receive these metrics, NGHE Solution may need to push alerts directly into Safety Services, creating tight coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct and only appropriate IM for this DP. Containment score of 0.92 is justified: metering aggregation, timestamp synchronization, ratio calculation, and API reporting are core NGHE responsibilities and do not belong in Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Vehicle and Teleoperation correctly own sensor hardware and raw data exposure; NGHE Solution owns the analytics and reporting layer. Minimal diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and reflects only necessary cross-boundary telemetry ingestion and API egress. No alternative module allocation would reduce diffusion without violating separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from the DP outputs. NGHE Solution will compute energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved via the data aggregation layer and report it via API. Verification requires: (1) calibration and validation of energy metering and work sensors; (2) confirmation that timestamp synchronization achieves required precision (recommend ±100 ms or better); (3) test harness to inject known energy and work values and verify ratio calculation; (4) operational monitoring over the agreed evaluation period to confirm metric stability and accuracy. Traceability from FR to DP to IM is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated as the IM owner. Responsibilities are well-defined: NGHE ingests raw data via Telemetry interface, synchronizes timestamps, aggregates across Vehicle and Teleoperation contexts, calculates ratios, and publishes via API. Vehicle and Teleoperation responsibilities are also clear: expose raw metering and work data with timestamps. No ambiguity or overlap detected. However, clarity depends on explicit interface contracts (Telemetry schema, timestamp format, data freshness guarantees) being documented and enforced. Recommend adding interface specification documents to the design baseline.

recommended_revisions: Specify timestamp synchronization protocol explicitly in DP2. Recommend NTP, GPS-based synchronization, or explicit handshake mechanism. Define acceptable clock drift tolerance (e.g., ±100 ms) and include validation in test plan.

Formalize Telemetry interface contract: define schema for raw energy metering data, work output sensor data, and timestamp format. Ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems commit to this contract and expose data with guaranteed freshness (e.g., within 1 second of measurement).

Standardize efficiency report API schema. Define a single canonical format for energy_per_ton, work_per_joule, and diagnostic metadata. Version the API and document backward compatibility policy to prevent diffusion of report formats across consumers.

Add secondary DP if real-time efficiency feedback is required for active control: 'DP2b: Deploy lightweight in-situ efficiency estimation in Vehicle/Teleoperation for fast feedback; NGHE Solution provides authoritative post-hoc validation and reporting.' This avoids latency issues while maintaining NGHE as the single source of truth for reported metrics.

Document SafetyAlert interface contract: if anomalous efficiency detection is enabled, specify the trigger criteria, alert schema, and handoff protocol to Safety Services. Ensure NGHE Solution does not directly manipulate Safety Services state; use event-based notification only.

Add acceptance test: inject known energy and work values into NGHE Solution and verify that computed energy_per_ton matches expected ratio within ±2% tolerance. Repeat across multiple Vehicle and Teleoperation operational modes to validate cross-system aggregation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM allocation is clear and well-contained, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results expose six critical diffusion risks and interface contract gaps that cannot be resolved at the current level of abstraction. These risks are architectural in nature and require explicit sub-level FRs to drive design decisions on timestamp synchronization, interface standardization, real-time feedback, and safety integration. Decomposing now prevents late-stage rework and ensures that interface contracts and synchronization protocols are designed with the same rigor as the core metering logic. Safety criticality is high, which elevates the importance of explicit interface and protocol specifications.
next_level_sub_frs: FR2.1: Synchronize timestamps across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution with bounded clock drift to enable reliable post-hoc energy-work pairing.
FR2.2: Expose raw energy metering and work output data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems via a standardized Telemetry interface contract with guaranteed data freshness and schema stability.
FR2.3: Compute and report energy_per_ton efficiency metric via a canonical, versioned API schema that prevents diffusion of report formats across external consumers.
FR2.4: Detect and alert on anomalous efficiency deviations via a clean event-based interface to Safety Services without direct state manipulation.
FR2.5: Provide real-time efficiency feedback to Vehicle and Teleoperation control loops (if required) while maintaining NGHE Solution as the authoritative post-hoc validator and reporter.

revise.why: DP2 is logically correct but under-specifies critical implementation bridges. Revise DP2 to explicitly reference sub-DPs for: (1) timestamp synchronization protocol (NTP, GPS, or handshake); (2) Telemetry interface schema and data freshness guarantees; (3) canonical efficiency report API schema with versioning policy; (4) SafetyAlert event-based notification contract; (5) optional in-situ feedback DP if real-time control is required. These sub-DPs will be derived from the next-level sub-FRs and will close the DP-IM bridge gaps identified in the diffusion risk analysis.

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems will reliably expose raw metering and work data via a Telemetry interface; if they do not, NGHE Solution will be forced to reach into subsystem internals, violating module boundaries.
Clock synchronization across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution is achievable within ±100 ms tolerance; if clock drift exceeds this, post-hoc ratio calculation will be unreliable.
External consumers (dashboards, control systems, analytics) will accept a single canonical efficiency report schema; if heterogeneous formats are demanded, NGHE Solution will face diffusion pressure.

Active control loops in Vehicle and Teleoperation do not require real-time (sub-second) efficiency feedback; if they do, a secondary in-situ metering DP will be needed, creating a secondary IM boundary.

Safety Services has or will adopt a clean event-based interface for receiving anomalous efficiency alerts; if not, NGHE Solution may need to push alerts directly into Safety Services, creating tight coupling.
Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is the primary success metric; secondary metrics (e.g., work_per_joule, efficiency trend analysis) are out of scope for this FR.
open_risks: Timestamp synchronization protocol not yet specified: Post-hoc aggregation depends on synchronized clocks. If NTP, GPS, or handshake mechanism is not selected and validated, clock drift will introduce systematic error into energy_per_ton calculation. Mitigation: FR2.1 will drive explicit protocol selection and tolerance specification.

Telemetry interface contract ambiguity: Vehicle and Teleoperation may expose data in heterogeneous formats or with inconsistent timestamps. If interface schema is not formalized, NGHE Solution will need to implement ad-hoc parsing and normalization, increasing coupling and maintenance burden. Mitigation: FR2.2 will drive standardized Telemetry schema and data freshness guarantees.

API schema diffusion: External consumers may demand custom report formats (JSON, CSV, Protobuf, etc.). If NGHE Solution accommodates all variants, reporting logic will diffuse across multiple code paths. Mitigation: FR2.3 will enforce a single canonical schema with versioning policy.

Real-time feedback latency: Post-hoc aggregation introduces latency (seconds to minutes). If Vehicle or Teleoperation control loops require real-time efficiency feedback for load balancing or power management, this DP alone is insufficient. Mitigation: FR2.5 will evaluate whether in-situ feedback is required and, if so, specify a secondary DP that maintains NGHE as the authoritative validator.

SafetyAlert coupling: If Safety Services does not have a clean event-based interface, NGHE Solution may need to directly manipulate Safety Services state, creating tight coupling and potential safety violations. Mitigation: FR2.4 will specify event-based notification contract and validate Safety Services readiness.

Verification latency: Acceptance test (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) may require weeks of operational monitoring. If early validation is needed, a synthetic test harness with injected energy and work values is required. Mitigation: Add acceptance test plan to FR2.3 sub-DP.
evidence_needed: Timestamp synchronization protocol specification: NTP configuration, GPS integration, or explicit handshake mechanism with clock drift tolerance (recommend ±100 ms) and validation procedure.

Telemetry interface schema document: JSON/Protobuf schema for raw energy metering data, work output sensor data, timestamp format, and data freshness guarantees (e.g., within 1 second of measurement).
Canonical efficiency report API schema: OpenAPI/Swagger specification for energy_per_ton, work_per_joule, diagnostic metadata, versioning policy, and backward compatibility guarantees.

SafetyAlert interface contract: event schema, trigger criteria for anomalous efficiency detection, and handoff protocol to Safety Services (event-based notification only, no direct state manipulation).

Real-time feedback feasibility study: analysis of Vehicle and Teleoperation control loop latency requirements; if sub-second feedback is required, specify secondary in-situ metering DP and reconciliation protocol with NGHE post-hoc validator.

Acceptance test plan: synthetic test harness to inject known energy and work values into NGHE Solution and verify computed energy_per_ton within ±2% tolerance across multiple Vehicle and Teleoperation operational modes.

Operational monitoring plan: procedure for collecting energy_per_ton metrics over agreed evaluation period (recommend 4-8 weeks) to confirm metric stability, accuracy, and compliance with E_target acceptance criterion.

Interface readiness assessment: confirmation from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services teams that they can commit to Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and other interface contracts by design baseline freeze date.
